channel.title: Climate Risk (Google Alerts)
channel.description: Últimos artigos de Climate Risk (Google Alerts)

item.title: Hail risk is shifting toward major farming regions as the climate warms - Earth.com
item.description:
A hailstorm is one of agriculture’s cruellest hazards. It arrives without much warning, lasts minutes, and can shred an entire wheat crop or bruise every piece of fruit on a tree while leaving the paddock next door untouched.

Farmers can lose a season’s work before they’ve had time to react. And unlike drought or heat, there’s essentially nothing to be done once hailstorms start.

A new study adds another layer of difficulty: the geography of hail risk is shifting.

As the planet warms, the atmospheric conditions that produce damaging hail are projected to move away from warmer regions and toward cooler ones – including south-eastern Australia, New Zealand, northern North America, and parts of Europe.

Within those regions, the risk is increasing not in summer, when many people associate severe storms, but in winter and shoulder seasons – exactly when many crops are in the ground.

The research was led by Dr Tim Raupach from the UNSW Institute of Climate Risk and Response.

Hailstones form when powerful updrafts in thunderstorms carry water droplets upward into freezing temperatures, where they accumulate layers of ice before eventually becoming too heavy to stay aloft.

The stronger the updraft, the larger the hailstone can grow before it falls.

Modelling future hail is notoriously difficult. Unlike temperature or rainfall, hail can’t be measured consistently across large areas – storms are brief, localized, and hard to observe systematically.

Rather than modeling hailstones directly, Raupach and his colleagues used three different proxies that capture the atmospheric conditions most associated with hail formation.

The three methods didn’t always agree, particularly in the tropics, which gives a sense of how uncertain future projections remain. What they did agree on, broadly, was a shift toward the poles.

“Under modeling scenarios of 2°C and 3°C of global warming, we see this overall shift towards more risk in cooler places and cooler times of the year,” Raupach said.

“Increasing risk in winter and often decreasing risk in summer – a shift from warmer to cooler regions and seasons.”

The reason hail behaves counterintuitively under warming is that higher temperatures push the atmosphere in two directions at once.

On one hand, a warmer atmosphere holds more energy, which can fuel stronger updrafts – the engine of hailstorm growth. Stronger updrafts can support larger hailstones.

On the other hand, warmer air raises the altitude at which ice starts to melt on its way down. Smaller hailstones that would have reached the ground in a cooler atmosphere simply melt before they get there.

“The atmosphere might be more prone to create storms, but the storms that are created might be less likely to have hail reach the ground,” Raupach said.

The net effect is an offsetting dynamic: there are more storms in some regions, but fewer of those storms are actually delivering hail at the surface. The concern is what happens when they do.

“Larger hailstones are more likely with stronger storm dynamics,” Raupach said. “That still has important implications for agriculture.”

The study examined 26 major crop types globally, looking at what proportion of each crop’s growing season fell under hail-prone conditions, and how that exposure is projected to change.

The signal for winter crops was clear and concerning.

In Australia, where wheat is the dominant winter crop, the most pronounced increases in hail-prone conditions show up in the south-east, from Tasmania up through the arc toward Melbourne and Sydney.

This region appears in both the historical trends and the future projections as a place where the hazard is growing.

A decrease in summer hail risk means little to a farmer with a winter crop. What matters is whether the risk is higher when the crop is in the ground.

For wheat growers in south-eastern Australia, the answer increasingly appears to be yes.

The findings also create an awkward problem for how we think about adapting agriculture to climate change more broadly.

As global warming makes traditional growing regions in lower latitudes hotter and drier, there’s an assumption that agriculture will shift poleward, into cooler areas that are becoming more viable.

What this study suggests is that some of those regions gaining agricultural potential are also gaining hail risk.

The opening of new growing zones and the increasing exposure to damaging storm conditions may, in some cases, cancel each other out.

For farmers, insurers, and policymakers, hail has always been difficult to plan around. You only need one severe event to lose a crop.

The uncertainty about future hail projections is real, and Raupach is honest about it. The regional picture is cleaner in some places than others, and the tropics remain particularly hard to model.

But the broad directional shift is, he says, something scientists can say with confidence.

“It’s hard. The uncertainty of it and the difficulty in getting at exactly what’s going on is one of the challenges that we face, and that decision-makers face,” he explained.

“But we can make broad statements. And the shift towards the poles is the broad statement we can make here.”

For a hazard that already receives less policy attention than drought, heatwaves, or floods – despite being just as capable of destroying a season’s work in minutes – that shift deserves more scrutiny than it has been getting.

The study is published in the journal Nature Climate Change.

—–

Like what you read? Subscribe to our newsletter for engaging articles, exclusive content, and the latest updates.

Check us out on EarthSnap, a free app brought to you by Eric Ralls and Earth.com.

—–

item.title: NZ's new forestry rules promise consistency. Will they also increase environmental risk?
item.description:
Analysis - One of the most important changes to New Zealand's environmental regulations in recent years came into force this week.

Yet outside policy circles, the forestry sector and a handful of councils, few people are likely to have noticed.

The government's freshly-amended National Environmental Standards for Commercial Forestry set bottom-line rules for activities ranging from afforestation and harvesting to slash management and erosion control.

There is a clear need for such safeguards.

Commercial forestry is one of New Zealand's most valuable industries, generating billions of dollars in export earnings and supporting thousands of jobs. So, it needs a regulatory framework that provides certainty for long-term investment and operational decisions.

But it can also have significant impacts on waterways, infrastructure, ecosystems and downstream communities if activities are not carefully managed, with recent years having provided some stark examples.

With the new rules now in effect, there are genuine questions over whether they strike the right balance - or might simply ease regulatory burden at significant ongoing environmental cost.

Storms, forestry slash: Where does legal responsibility lie?

A solution in search of a problem?

The government says the reforms will make forestry regulation more consistent across New Zealand while reducing compliance costs. They are also intended to focus regulatory effort on higher-risk activities and locations.

Key changes include narrowing the circumstances in which councils can impose stricter local rules, replacing some prescriptive slash-management requirements with a risk-based assessment process and reducing planning requirements for afforestation and replanting.

When proposed last year as part of the government's wider resource management programme, the amendments quickly drew criticism from environmental groups and others.

Critics argued the reforms did little to address the scale of modern clear-fell harvesting - known to raise the risk of forestry-related damage - or increasing severe storms that exceed the "one-in-20-year" threshold on which many forestry rules are based.

'A storm every eight days' - country's biggest insurer calls for systemic response

There were also concerns about limiting councils' ability to introduce stronger local safeguards, allowing forestry activities in high-risk areas and enabling more industry self-regulation, despite recent cases highlighting its flaws.

There are now concerns this narrowed set of rules will not effectively reduce the the risk of slash and sediment entering waterways in heavy storms, as disastrously occurred during Cyclone Gabrielle in 2023.

There also remain serious questions around the evidence base for the reforms - and the process through which they were developed.

For instance, the government's regulatory impact statement, used to assess the costs, benefits and rationale for the amendments, made no reference to an independent 2024 review of councils' use of forestry rules.

The review, which the responsible ministers had received, found most councils had not set about developing their own stricter rules and instead were relying largely on older planning provisions.

This finding is important because it was at odds with the reforms' premise that stringent council rules have been imposing unnecessary costs and uncertainty on foresters.

In its submission on the reforms, the Environmental Defence Society argued a better solution would have been to require councils to develop stronger and more consistent protections for vulnerable rivers, lakes, estuaries and communities.

That proposal was not pursued. Instead, the amendments constrained the circumstances in which councils can impose stricter rules, largely limiting them to areas classified as severely erosion-prone.

Yet forestry-related risks do not neatly align with such definitions, as damage from landslides, slash and sediment can still occur in other landscapes - particularly during intense rainfall.

What the reforms leave out

Compounding concerns about the reforms is a government-imposed "plan stop" that bars councils from making any planning changes until the end of 2027.

This move, designed to prevent councils from spending time and money on plan provisions that may not survive under a new resource management system, is likely to further curb local efforts to manage forestry-related risk.

More realistically, it may be the 2030s before councils are able to revisit these issues. By then, the new system will still be bedding in and proposed local government reorganisation may have further delayed action.

As well, councils could be required to compensate land owners for tougher rules by a proposed "regulatory relief" regime.

The reforms are also notable for what they leave out.

They came with little discussion of how forestry risks are managed in comparable overseas jurisdictions - or even whether New Zealand's regulatory settings remain fit for a future of more frequent and severe storms. At a time public finances are already under pressure, these damaging events are only growing more costly.

To address this risk, the forestry consultation process could have led to stronger standards to safeguard steep headwater catchments and gullies that can generate large volumes of slash and sediment. These opportunities were ultimately missed.

Concerns also remain over a new process for assessing risk from slash which relies heavily on industry-led assessments, and provides limited scope for councils to decline or call for independent scrutiny by geotechnical experts.

Ultimately, the issue is larger than forestry regulation alone. As climate change intensifies rainfall and erosion hazards, the stability of New Zealand's hill country becomes a matter of economic resilience as well as environmental protection.

* Steve Urlich is a senior lecturer in environmental management at Lincoln University.

- This story was originally published onThe Conversation.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: World Bank Group Supports Resilient Municipal Infrastructure to Modernize Cities in Türkiye
item.description:
WASHINGTON DC, June 5th, 2026—The World Bank Group approved a EUR 191.5 million (US$219.4 million) loan to Türkiye to modernize urban infrastructure and services in the fast-growing cities of Antalya and Konya, addressing a growing demand for public transport, clean water and sanitation, and efficient energy systems, while generating jobs along the way.

The Green and Future Cities Project will be implemented through İller Bankası A.Ş. (ILBANK) with the guarantee of the Republic of Türkiye. Türkiye’s rapid urbanization has intensified demand for efficient and resilient municipal infrastructure. While fast-growing cities are central to the country’s economy, many face constraints in accessing long-term financing for major investments to support people and growing economic activities.

“Türkiye’s cities are key drivers of economic growth and job creation but also face increasing pressures from rapid urbanization and climate change,” said J. Humberto Lopez, World Bank Country Director for Türkiye. “This project will help municipalities invest in modern, resilient infrastructure while strengthening their capacity to plan and finance sustainable urban development.”

Planned investments include the expansion and modernization of public transport systems, such as tramlines and low-emission vehicles, as well as upgrades to water supply, wastewater treatment, and sanitation infrastructure. The project will also support measures to enhance energy efficiency and strengthen climate adaptation and resilience. These investments are expected to improve urban mobility, enhance environmental sustainability, and raise the quality of life for residents, while contributing to economic growth and job creation.

The project will also provide technical assistance to ILBANK and participating municipalities to strengthen their capacity in project preparation, financial management, and sustainable urban planning. This will include developing pipelines of bankable, climate-smart investments, strengthen municipal financial and institutional capacity, and enhance long-term resilience to climate and disaster risks.

“By combining financing with technical expertise, this project will help cities develop bankable, climate-smart investments and improve their access to long-term financing,” said Ahmet Kindap, Task Team Leader for of the Project.

The project is also designed to help municipalities strengthen their creditworthiness and lay the groundwork for greater private sector participation over time.

Aligned with Türkiye’s national development priorities, the project will support both mitigation and adaptation efforts. Investments are expected to reduce greenhouse gas emissions, enhance energy efficiency, and strengthen resilience to climate-related risks such as flooding, drought, and extreme heat. By improving infrastructure systems and service delivery, the project will help cities better withstand future shocks while promoting sustainable and inclusive urban growth.

This project preparation benefited from technical assistance and grants from the Global Facility for Disaster Reduction and Recovery (GFDRR)’s Japan-World Bank Program for Mainstreaming Disaster Risk Management in Developing Countries, supported by the Government of Japan.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Syria addresses climate challenges with national plans to protect resources
item.description:
Damascus, June 5 (SANA) Syria is continuing to implement national programs aimed at addressing environmental and climate challenges, strengthening the sustainable management of natural resources , and supporting long-term development, officials said on World Environment Day.

Speaking to SANA on Friday, Anas Al-Rahmoun, director of environmental awareness at the Ministry of Local Administration and Environment, said Syria faces a range of environmental challenges linked to climate change, including declining water resources, prolonged drought periods, ecosystem degradation and growing pressure on productive sectors that depend on natural resources, particularly agriculture.

Al-Rahmoun said these challenges have become a central focus of national environmental policies, with government institutions working to develop programs that enhance resilience to climate change and mitigate its economic and social impacts.

He said the ministry has placed climate adaptation and natural resource protection among its top priorities through specialized studies and projects aimed at integrating climate considerations into development planning in line with international sustainable development goals.

Assess Risks of Environmental Change

As part of efforts to strengthen the scientific basis of environmental policymaking, the ministry has launched a national climate risk assessment project in cooperation with the United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) and a number of national institutions.
The study aims to identify major climate risks facing different sectors, assess their potential impacts and provide scientific foundations for national adaptation policies and environmental risk management strategies.

Access to International Climate Finance

Al-Rahmoun said the ministry is also working to improve institutional readiness to access international climate finance mechanisms, including the Green Climate Fund, the Adaptation Fund, the Global Environment Facility and the Loss and Damage Fund.
He noted that national projects have been aligned with international financing requirements to increase opportunities for securing technical and financial support for priority environmental initiatives in Syria.

National Priority Projects

Among the ministry’s key initiatives is a national priority projects program designed to identify and develop climate-related projects in sectors most vulnerable to environmental change.
According to Al-Rahmoun, the program was developed through a series of technical meetings and workshops involving government institutions, experts and stakeholders to establish intervention priorities based on national needs and international climate standards.

Waste Management

The ministry is also preparing a national strategy for solid waste management in cooperation with international partners, aiming to move from temporary solutions toward an integrated and sustainable system covering waste collection, sorting, treatment, safe disposal and recycling.
The strategy seeks to improve environmental and public health conditions, reduce the impact of uncontrolled dumping sites and strengthen the capacity of local authorities to manage sanitation and waste services.

Protecting Natural Resources

Al-Rahmoun said forest protection and the preservation of vegetation cover remain important components of environmental policy because of their role in protecting biodiversity, combating desertification and strengthening communities’ resilience to climate change.
He added that reforestation programs, environmental awareness campaigns and efforts to protect natural resources are being pursued in coordination with relevant institutions as part of broader sustainable development goals.

National Workshops

The ministry has also organized a series of national workshops and technical consultations involving government agencies, universities, scientific institutions, international organizations and environmental experts to support policy development and strengthen coordination on climate and environmental issues.

Environmental Awareness

Al-Rahmoun stressed that environmental awareness remains a shared responsibility and that the success of environmental policies depends not only on government action but also on active public participation.
He said awareness campaigns and educational programs targeting different age groups are being implemented to promote environmental responsibility, sustainable resource use and environmentally friendly practices.
World Environment Day, observed annually on June 5, serves as an opportunity to renew commitments to environmental protection and strengthen efforts to address climate challenges through effective policies, priority projects and expanded national and international cooperation.

MHD
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Ranking Member Maxine Waters Urges Chair Hill to Call Immediate Hearing with FHFA ...
item.description:
Today, Congresswoman Maxine Waters (D-CA), the top Democrat on the House Financial Services Committee, sent a letter to Congressman French Hill (R-AR), Chair of the Committee, demanding an immediate hearing with Federal Housing Finance Agency (FHFA) Director Bill Pulte following President Trump’s decision to install Pulte as Acting Director of National Intelligence (DNI) while he continues serving as FHFA Director. In the letter, Ranking Member Waters raised serious concerns about Pulte’s lack of qualifications to oversee the nation’s Intelligence Community (IC) while simultaneously managing the nation’s housing finance system amid a worsening housing affordability crisis.

“For numerous reasons, this appointment is terrible for America. From prioritizing and rewarding political weaponization of critical government positions and data to minimizing the intensifying affordable housing crisis, which is squeezing the wallets of American families, Pulte’s disinterested and substandard service in the Administration is already highly problematic,” wrote Ranking Member Waters.

Waters goes on to detail how Pulte has abused his position at FHFA through reckless actions that include unlawfully declaring himself chairman of both Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac; using his position to launch baseless, politically motivated attacks and undermine the independence of the Federal Reserve; rolling back climate-risk and minority homeownership initiatives; eliminating programs designed to expand access to credit for historically disadvantaged communities; and weakening affordable housing goals in ways that could push homeownership further out of reach for working families. She also criticized Pulte for promoting risky 50-year mortgage products that could trap families in debt for decades while slowing opportunities to build generational wealth through homeownership.

Waters added:

“The role of director of FHFA is not a part-time job, and Members of HFSC must know, before he takes on his new, significantly broader and more sensitive duties at the helm of the IC, whether he will continue to put President Trump’s petty grievances ahead of the pressing issues of the housing market.”

In closing, Ranking Member Waters urged Chairman Hill to exercise the Committee’s oversight authority to immediately require FHFA Director Pulte—who has yet to appear before the Committee—to  testify under oath regarding his actions at FHFA, his ability to simultaneously oversee both agencies, and the impact his leadership will have on housing affordability, financial stability, and public trust in government institutions.

See the letter HERE.

###
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado
